main_ingredients: tomato base or satay sauce base

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard individual pizza of approximately 350 g, equivalent to roughly 8 slices, as typically served at a Singapore café-restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Pizza with vegetables (SR Legacy #21299) and Peanut sauce / satay sauce component (USDA SR Legacy #16397 peanut butter); lion's mane mushroom nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2346406
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for lion's mane satay sauce pizza in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by component modelling: pizza dough and cheese base from USDA SR Legacy vegetable pizza (FDC 21299), satay/peanut sauce contribution from USDA SR Legacy peanut butter (FDC 16397), and lion's mane mushroom from USDA FoodData Central FDC 2346406. Proportions estimated as approximately 55% pizza base with cheese, 25% satay sauce, 20% lion's mane mushroom topping. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a restaurant-prepared fusion pizza of this type.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pizza and pizza-type foods — analogous estimate based on 'Pizza, cheese' GI = 60 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lion's mane satay sauce pizza. GI of 60 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cheese pizza (GI = 60, medium category), which represents the closest analogous food. The satay sauce component (peanut-based, low GI ~14) would moderately lower the overall GI, but given the dominant pizza dough base, GI 60 is retained as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 103.25) / 100 ≈ 62.0, based on 350 g serving with 29.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per full pizza serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this pizza due to its high sodium content from satay sauce, cheese, and processed dough; a single serving may provide approximately 79% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 60) and high glycemic load per serving (~62) from refined pizza dough base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this pizza in small portions due to its medium GI and high per-serving glycemic load; pairing with a fibre-rich salad and choosing a thin-crust variant may help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) from cheese and peanut-based satay sauce, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit this pizza to occasional consumption, as the combination of saturated fat from cheese and high sodium from satay sauce may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lion's Mane Mushroom Satay Sauce Pizza is a Singapore-originated fusion pizza that combines a Western pizza format with Southeast Asian flavour profiles. The pizza features a baked wheat-flour dough base topped with a peanut-based satay sauce in place of or alongside a conventional tomato base, generous portions of lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) — a prized culinary and functional mushroom known for its seafood-like texture and mild umami flavour — along with mozzarella cheese, onion, and fresh coriander. The satay sauce contributes a rich, nutty, mildly spiced sweetness characteristic of Southeast Asian satay cuisine, while the lion's mane mushroom provides a meaty, slightly chewy texture that makes this pizza suitable for vegetarians. The dish is baked at high temperature until the crust is golden and the cheese is melted and lightly browned. Nutritionally, this pizza provides moderate energy (~248 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from cheese and mushroom, and moderate fat (~10.2 g per 100 g) with a notable peanut-derived unsaturated fat contribution. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to satay sauce and cheese, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 60 (medium), driven primarily by the refined wheat dough base. Lion's mane mushroom is recognised in functional food research for potential neuroprotective and immunomodulatory properties, adding a wellness dimension to this dish. This pizza is served at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall, Singapore, and represents the growing trend of Asian-fusion pizza in Singapore's diverse food scene.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for lion's mane satay sauce pizza exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) or USDA FoodData Central. All nutrient values are estimated by component modelling from USDA SR Legacy analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database cheese pizza entry. Confidence is therefore low; values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a direct HPB Foodo entry if this dish is added to the Singapore food composition database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-modelled estimates; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-database entry exists for this specific dish. Values carry inherent uncertainty of approximately ±15–20% for macronutrients. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this food type in the component sources used; lion's mane mushroom may contain trace vitamin D2 when UV-exposed but this is not quantified in the USDA entries used. 3. Trans fat (0.1 g per 100 g) reflects a small residual from cheese; this is naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) rather than industrial trans fat. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component sources as described in nutrient_source; these are reasonable estimates but not confirmed by direct measurement. 5. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' which is incorrect for a pizza dish; corrected to 'Mixed' as this is a composite prepared food. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's apparent positioning; this should be verified against MUIS certification. 7. GI glycemic load per serving: (60 × 103.25 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 62.0. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not directly reported in the USDA component sources used for estimation; specifically vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used.
